query: Now I'd like to start the design session for col-020.  This was originally scoped in @product/research/ass-018/MILESTONE-PROPOSAL.md for full details.  Before we begin, I want to have a quick discussion of value of this feature
before you make this asseessment, do a context_retrospective for the last feature we implemented, nxs-010, then come back and have this discussion

so... ultimately, I think this cross 'session' analysis WAS an issue, however, I actually think it was closed by earlier work.  We have to be realistic.. Unimatrix knows exactly WHAT happened, but it does not have the context to understand if it was really good, bad, or by design.  I think we architecturally need to stay away from that, until such time we want to expand the scope of Unimatrix.  I don't want to do things in Unimatrix that are 'attempts'... I want ALL features to be GREAT. . \
\
That said.. take a look at issue 91.  I think THIS is an area that we SHOULD focus on, as a priority, and possibly slot nicely in to our current capabilitys.  Read and provide thoughts
Now read the Wave 3 features from @product-vision and the @product/research/ass-018/MILESTONE-PROPOSAL.md for details... Lets make sure we get the timing of issue 91 correct..

ugh... now I'm relooking at this... So, the 1 new calculation I like in col-020 is knowledge reuse..., the others I think we may already be abe to calculate, or maybe not have as much meaning as we think.. (although, it is possible that the LLM is doing the analysis, instead of Unimatrix doing it as a summary today, that would have value)... but I do see issue 91 being maybe the FIRST feature we delivery in Wave 3 from @product/PRODUCT-VISION.md .  So... tell me.. you listed metrics that came out of the retrospective... did you calculate them, or did Unimatrix? If you had to calculate those, then I'd say col020 stays where it is, and we do Issue 91 immediately afterwards...
I want you to update @product-vision to add vnc-011 (009 is already been taken) as the first feature in Wave 3.  Leave a reference to this gh issue in the description.
OK... now commit, check git status for stray untracked changes, clean them up, commit them, and then create a PR to merge

it is now time to begin design of col-020 from @product/PRODUCT-VISION.md and @product/research/ass-018/MILESTONE-PROPOSAL.md .  Lets talk about meaningful metrics this adds that we don't already have available

well... here's where context matters (this may not be context Unimatrix has or exposes... yet).  In my workflow, each feature will currently have at least 2 sessions.  1 for design, and 1 for delivery. they have separate purposes, will always look different from each other.  Comparing design sesseions across features can have value, but comparing session 1 that creates artifacts, and session 2 that reads artifacts and creates code/tests.. not so meaningful as a raw metric.  The knowledge reuse is GOLD, if we do it right.  It sells the value proposition of Unimatrix on every feature.

hmm... well, MY protocol there is clear delineation... i can tell you that... but to do this right, Unimatrix as a product, it would need to be able to DETECT the nature of session boundaries (if they were consistent, and if not.. thats an observation in and of itself).  I'm not sure in all honesty, we're quite ready for that

so.. is it possible to start reporting on the summarized session focus?  I think we have the data... might be interesting, and a predecessor to to being able to compare common sessions... I think this might be what your topic_session_summary starts to get out??
yes... I like it... I think there is still some room for discussion of the knowledge_reuse_rate in some detail.. this is the key one in this feature

so... I think we're going to have to focus on the key benefits we're shooting for.  Primary, was actually long term knowledge retention for the project... so, context_search followed by a context_lookup is a signal if found something useful, ANY signals coming in has high value, The injection log is actually a little more opaque.. how do we know what we provided was valuable??  difficult.  The ONLY thing I could think of there is if we were also returning a confidence measure in that response that we could see.  Again, I don't want to build anything specific to my protocol, but alot of what we build that is truly feature specific goes into files, not unimatrix... so we have to account for that is still a good option

yes.. the only other aspect... Unimatrix has some predefined categories... is displaying the types of knowledge it considered useful... also is actionalable (maybe their agent instructions have gaps in some areas).. Unimatrix should help humans improve over time
ready to go into design... I like it.

Q1: can we do an 'or' match?  again.. trying to not be TOO opinionated on the analysis as much as we can be.; Q2: at this time no.  This is an architectual decision, that we'll revisit when/if we have additional capabilities to assess value and/or confidence metric. Q3: all active entries.  Doesn't matter where the knowledge came from if its valuable, Q4: agree, Q5: agree

you are technically correct, that case-sensitive is a more correct matching method, but if a future change simply capitalizes Rework, I wouldn't want to make our analysis that brittle.  This is more about trying to capture the semantic intent than data updates... case-insensitive is correct in this case.
it is time to pickup the completed design for col-020 and move to implementation

approved to merge
now, rebuild the application for release, and kill all running unimatrix-server processes
test unimatrix server is running
now run /retro con col-020 and share specifically what col-020 added to the reporting

go back and check the requirements of col-020 vs what we delivered.  I specifically directed us NOT to focus on the session 1 -> session 2 reuse, and instead focus on ANY context search -> lookup patterns of ANY data.  Are you indicating that there was 0 search done against unimatrix at all, or did we not build the capabiilty to focus on ANY search and delivery of data to agents in col-020?

so, yeah.. not sure how that got messed up.  I was specific in my directin and said I'm NOT that interested in session 1->2, but I WAS very interested in any pattern that did a search then lookup... now that I'm thinking about it really ANY direct lookup I can argue likely had some value to the agents that they would have otherwise either not had, or taken alot more tokens to find. I'd like you to consider 1. Should these bugs be handled together? Based on that response, create either 1 or 2 gh issues with the details provided.  Stop afterwards for the next step.  Be sure to be thorough in the data you place in these issues, other agents will likely fix them
lets talk about issue #2 further... isn't knowledge_in/out the same as what I'm pushing issue 2 to become?  Lets think objecctively

OK.. I see.  The names chosen: knowledge_in, knowledge_reuse are not very intuitive to me.  knowledge_in to me is more like knowledge_served, and knowledge_resuse, is more like feature_knowledge_reuse?  I see the value of separate metrics... the first is overall Unimatrix value prop, and the second is opportunities for human to enhance their processes

Since we're in this space... knowledge stored... I like this.. there is also a knowledge udpdated (we can deprecate, quarantine, superseed)... also part of the value prop... any way to easily include this?

OK.. now, we have a fairly fully functional unimatrix... long term memory for this platform.  In some ways, I believe we are using this well, in others, I feel like our process could better take advantage of the capabilities we've built.  I have ideas, but I'd like you to trace through our Claude.md, protocols, agent definitions, and skills, and analyze a few different ways we could better take advantage of this platform.  Your goal is not to find ways to USE unimatrix... it is to find ways that using Unimatrix better, could improve delivery, improving long term memory of patterns, procedures, reducing token consumption, or any other way you can think of.

just ran retrospective on vnc-011, and col-020b before that... the data it found for those 2 latest features was EXTREMElY sparce, where col-020, was chock full.  I blieeve it may be possible that something in col-020b may have corrupted the full data flow driving our retro analysis.  I don't believe with the size and scope of vnc-011 for example, this was all the data there is: RETROSPECTIVE COMPLETE — Knowledge base updated.                                                                                                                                         
                                                                                                                                                                                           
  Feature: vnc-011                                                                                                                                                                         
  PR: #197 (merged)                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                                                                                                                           
  Retrospective summary:                                                                                                                                                                   
  - Sessions: 1, Tool calls: 11 (primary agent only — subagents did the work), Duration: 1h 50m
  - Hotspots: 0
  - Baseline outliers: 0

  Knowledge extracted:
  - Patterns: 1 new (#949 — Domain-Specific Markdown Formatter Module Pattern)
  - Procedures: 0
  - Lessons: 1 new (#953 — Human overrides must propagate to ALL downstream artifacts)
  - ADRs stored: 3 (#950 format-dependent evidence_limit, #951 deterministic selection, #952 separate module)
  - ADRs validated: 2 (ADR-002, ADR-003)
  - ADRs with caveats: 1 (ADR-001 — file text says unwrap_or(0), shipped code correctly uses unwrap_or(3) per human override)

  Gate 3a lesson: Pseudocode agents consumed stale ADR text that predated the human override. The implementation brief had the correct value, but agents prioritized architecture
  artifacts. Future mitigation: ensure human overrides are explicitly noted in ALL artifacts agents will read, not just the brief.

just run mcp call to unimatrix context_retrospective

what does this mean? The topic signal system (col-017) already does text-based feature extraction from tool inputs — it parses things like product/features/col-020/
so, I thought subagents used the same sessionID of the primary agent, so is there really a SessionStart for subagents?
well... I'm still confused why the col-017 worked for a couple features, then stopped working.  if feels like the heuristic applied... may no longer be getting applied

now I understand.  Here's what happened ( I think)  The previous iterations where thi was working, the primary claude agent, would spawn the scrum-master/coordinator agent... who then spawned all specialist agents.   The implication, was that the Scrum-master ALWAYS got sessionClose. . 2 days ago, Claude pushed a change, that eliminated the double -spawn model, and I had to convert the primary agent to be the scrum master.  So the SM used to get spawned with specific attributes, a name, etc... now, the primary agent is just assuming the role.  I think that explains the difference.. bcause I'm alwasy runngin retro from the primary agent that ran the implementation

agreed.  not sure how to run it more eagerly though, giving enough time/traffic for consensus to find something, and prevent failure.  Also have to account for times that I end the session after design, and start a new session for implementation... same feature.. so for every session, something needs to trigger this?  Thoughts?
how large is this... is it small enough to treat as bugfix?  create issue, start bugfix process?

good feedback.  1. We purposefully shy'd away from worklow entries in Unimatrix. Although I like the concept, it becomes harder for the human to visualize only in a database.  However, some of what you say is right.    here's a little of an uphill battle... getting agents to voluntarialy provide feedback... the right kind of feedback.  I do like the comment about making stewardship of the data mandatory and universal... Need to find the right way to inject this.  Are skills too heavyweight?  I'm actually wondering about a change if anyone does a specific lookup on 1 item (as opposed to search) should that automatically boost confidence?
I'm not sure about that.
approve.  lets move forware
for now, through this question with the research in a gh issue, then lets get baack to how we might go about approaching a more holistic approach to addressing our full unimatrix integration
1. So the #reusable Findings model is interesting... but then why would they not simply use unimatrix to store them directly?

I've seen other systems create a memory layer in between agents... In theory Unimatrix could be that here too, some of that is not long term memory data so I've been hesitant to use it for agent-agent transport, but what you're suggesting is not that.  What do you thik about rust-developer?

the meta model honestly for the rust dev is possibly by crate as topic and use pattern/convention categories.  A little bit of a challenge is, these change frequently, and claude is so used to searching the codebase itself... thoughts on that?

I actually like this alot... the retro architect can deprecate low-value patterns.  using the current validation steps to also validate knowledge base, and specific instructions on how to store the dev patterns (likely is going to have to be a skill for consistency) may make sense.  \
\
In reality, in the back of my mind... future phase, I want to export this to other repositories, and I want it to be fairly easy to get up and running.  Defining a working set of skills to deploy in some new repository and recommendations for use is likely a part of that

I'm not sure the pseudocode agent should store patterns, they might, though lookup patterns by component.  2. yes, 3. yes, with some direction to identify what could have been done during design to prevent.  Also.. many times they know which feature caused an issue... the linkage of outcome/rework to the feature that caused it. 4. well... each of the other categores seem to have their own skill.  it also reduces the size of the skill, and is more self evidient that the agent is supposed to do just by the skill name.   Trying to get they agents to get the benefits Unimatrix promises is your #1 task, your #2 task, is still to be cognizent of context windows for agents, and not overbloat them with instructions.  This is a judgement call.  For this iteration error on better descriptions where agents are storing/updating entries.  Quality of updates matters.
agree with your recommendations
do we currently have any explicit helpful/unhelpful response feedback?  Or is this a CLAUDE.MD level instruction?
option 2 is preferred... however 'next call'?  they have to identify the item that was helpful.. in the skills is the right place.  This is an addition, because I don't think its currently in scope?

You are researching the problem space for nan-003.
Your agent ID: nan-003-researcher

High-level intent: nan-003 delivers two skills for onboarding new repos to Unimatrix:

1. `/unimatrix-init` — Deterministic setup:
   - Append a Unimatrix block to CLAUDE.md with awareness of available skills (unimatrix- prefix), category conventions, and basic usage instructions
   - Scan existing .claude/agents/ definitions and recommend changes (add context_briefing orientation, outcome reporting, reference unimatrix-* skills) — READ-ONLY recommendations, no auto-editing
   - Must be idempotent — safe to run twice

2. `/unimatrix-seed` — Optional, conversational repo exploration:
   - Agent explores repo structure: directory layout, README, package manifests, config files
   - Identifies modules, goals, patterns, conventions at a high level
   - Stores foundational knowledge entries via context_store
   - Presents human with options for deeper exploration
   - Human controls depth: high-level pass is automatic, deeper dives require explicit opt-in

Key design decisions already made:
- Skill naming: all production skills use unimatrix- prefix
- Three-layer chain: CLAUDE.md awareness -> skill invocation -> agent behavior
- Wiring NOT in scope (nan-004): settings.json, hooks, binary installation, ONNX model download
- No auto-editing of agent files: recommendations only
- Seed depth is human-controlled

Existing context:
- alc-001 research identified the CLAUDE.md block + agent orientation + skills pattern
- uni-init bootstrap prototype produced 67 low-quality entries — this conversational, human-directed approach is the fix
- Existing skills in .claude/skills/ are the reference for skill file format
- Current skills: store-adr, review-pr, retro, query-patterns, store-pattern, store-procedure, store-lesson, record-outcome, knowledge-lookup, knowledge-search, uni-git

Dependencies:
- Assumes MCP server is already wired (nan-004 or manual setup)
- Skills are files in .claude/skills/ — they need to be in the target repo
- Skills call MCP tools (context_store, context_search, context_briefing, etc.) which must be available

this is good enough... so the model in my head, our current design does not go away... maybe it gets less eager... and only replaces a null value, not one that has already been filled by cycle_begin.  cycle_end is nice to have just like sessionStop. . <result>The research is complete. Here is a summary of what was done and the key findings:

---

## ASS-020 Research Complete

Four files written to `/workspaces/unimatrix/product/research/ass-020/`:
- `FINDINGS.md` — 15 prioritized findings with file:line citations
- `MUTEX-AUDIT.md` — Complete map of every `lock_conn()` call, hold time, and frequency
- `TICK-AUDIT.md` — Full tick breakdown with spawn_blocking counts and timing estimates
- `RECOMMENDATIONS.md` — Tactical fixes and architectural recommendations

Summary posted to GH issue #270.

---

## Critical Findings

**P1 (Critical)**: Two naked `.unwrap()` calls at `services/status.rs:638, 657` can permanently kill the background tick task on a JoinError. The tick loop's error handler at `background.rs:260` does not catch async task panics — once dead, the tick never restarts.

**P2 (Critical)**: The tick runs three sequential 120-second windows (`maintenance_tick` → `SupersessionState rebuild` → `extraction_tick`), each independently timed. Total potential tick duration: 6 minutes. During any mutex-holding phase, concurrent MCP requests are blocked indefinitely.

**P3 (Critical)**: `spawn_blocking_with_timeout` (30s) exists at `infra/timeout.rs` and is applied only to `context_retrospective`. Every other hot-path tool — search, store, briefing, status — uses bare `spawn_blocking` with no timeout. When the tick holds the mutex, these tasks block indefinitely, triggering the client timeout + reconnect cycle that manifests as the ~15 minute idle drop.

**P4 (High)**: The contradiction scan in `compute_report()` Phase 2 (`status.rs:424–479`) re-embeds every active entry via ONNX and runs HNSW search per entry on **every maintenance tick**. O(N) ONNX calls, not rate-limited.

**Root cause of regression**: crt-014 (+2–5s), crt-018b (+10–20s), and crt-019 (+3–8s) each added independently manageable costs. Combined, at current scale they push the tick to 40–90 seconds. At 3-5× volume they will routinely exceed the 120s timeout. Any I/O slowness (disk, SQLite WAL checkpoint) pushes them over today — explaining why the instability is intermittent rather than consistent.</result>

**Output:** `/workspaces/unimatrix/product/research/ass-020/SECURITY-AUDIT.md`
**GH comment:** https://github.com/dug-21/unimatrix/issues/270#issuecomment-4062955880

**Summary of findings:**

Two Critical findings dominate the risk profile:

1. `PERMISSIVE_AUTO_ENROLL=true` at `infra/registry.rs:27` — every anonymous MCP agent receives Write capability. The code comment explicitly says "In production this should be false." Introduced by PR #228, never reverted.

2. `context_briefing` output has no `[KNOWLEDGE DATA]` framing in any response format (`mcp/response/briefing.rs:62–75`). Briefing is automatically injected into agent context at session start, making every stored convention and ADR a live prompt injection vector. This is the OWASP ASI06 surface in its most direct form.

Three High findings: `context_retrospective` has no `require_cap()` guard (`tools.rs:1103`); read tools (search/lookup/get) also lack output framing (`entries.rs`); background tick bypasses SecurityGateway entirely via direct `store.insert()` (`background.rs:1023`).

The highest-ROI remediation path covers P1+P2+P3 with three small code changes (one constant flip, one function call addition, two response formatter additions). The full audit includes 10 findings across 5 security layers with file:line citations, intentional relaxation analysis, and a prioritized 10-item security debt backlog.</result>

ASS-020 (security audit) identified that the current PERMISSIVE_AUTO_ENROLL = true + per-call agent_id self-reporting combination makes the capability system vestigial: any caller passes agent_id: \"human\" to get full access, and there is no way to prevent it on stdio transport.

The audit's Staged Identity Model describes a near-term bridge that eliminates both problems without waiting for OAuth/HTTPS:

"The env-var bridge eliminates the 'human everywhere' problem. The MCP server reads UNIMATRIX_SESSION_AGENT at startup as the session default; tool calls can override per-call if needed."
A post-audit design session confirmed the approach and the decision to disable permissive auto-enroll, replacing it with env-var-configured session identity.

Design

Stage 2 — Env-var Bridge (this issue)

Mechanism    Detail
UNIMATRIX_SESSION_AGENT    Env var in settings.json MCP config. Read at server startup. Becomes the default agent_id for all tool calls that omit agent_id.
PERMISSIVE_AUTO_ENROLL    Convert from compile-time const to env-var-controlled (default false). Flip to true for local dev only, not the default.
Session agent enrollment    At startup, if UNIMATRIX_SESSION_AGENT is set and the agent is not enrolled, auto-enroll it with [Read, Write, Search]. One enrollment, not per-call.
Hook differentiation    UDS hook calls continue to use \"hook\" / \"background\" as their source identities (already differentiated at the transport layer — no change needed).
What this achieves

No per-call agent_id burden on callers — zero friction
No \"human\" workaround needed
Identity is now configured in one place (settings.json) and reflects the actual LLM session
PERMISSIVE_AUTO_ENROLL = false means unknown/unnamed agents get [Read, Search] only — Write requires a configured identity
Same enrollment pipeline and capability gates; only the binding point for identity changes
Forward-compatible: when HTTPS/OAuth arrives, UNIMATRIX_SESSION_AGENT slot is replaced by token claims — no structural changes
Scope


Read UNIMATRIX_SESSION_AGENT env var at server startup

Use as default agent_id in resolve_or_enroll() when per-call agent_id is absent or empty

Auto-enroll session agent with [Read, Write, Search] at startup if not already enrolled

Convert PERMISSIVE_AUTO_ENROLL from compile-time const to env-var (UNIMATRIX_PERMISSIVE_ENROLL, default false)

Update settings.json documentation / example to show UNIMATRIX_SESSION_AGENT configuration

Fix [infra-001] 3 tools tests expect Write rejection but PERMISSIVE_AUTO_ENROLL grants Write #233 and [infra-001] test_multi_agent_interaction: restricted agent can now store after bugfix-228 permissive auto-enroll #238 — tests that assert Write rejection for unknown agents are now correct; permissive behavior should no longer be the default
Files

crates/unimatrix-server/src/infra/registry.rs — PERMISSIVE_AUTO_ENROLL, resolve_or_enroll()
crates/unimatrix-server/src/main.rs or server init — env var read + startup enrollment
crates/unimatrix-server/src/mcp/tools.rs — default agent_id injection from session config
Integration tests — update to reflect PERMISSIVE_AUTO_ENROLL = false default
References

ASS-020 security audit: product/research/ass-020/SECURITY-AUDIT.md §Staged Identity Model

we aligned on a Vision/Story for what we think Unimatrix will become:  This needs to be integrated into the @product/research/ass-022/06-roadmap-to-ubiquity.md : Vision:
  ▎ Unimatrix is a self-learning knowledge integrity engine. It captures knowledge that emerges from doing work — in any domain — and makes it trustworthy, correctable, and
  ever-improving. It delivers the right knowledge at the right time.

  ---
  Story:

  Unimatrix began in agentic software delivery, where the problem was specific: AI agents forget, contradict each other, and confidently repeat mistakes. We built a knowledge engine
  where nothing is merely stored — everything is attributed, hash-chained for integrity, scored by real usage, and correctable with full provenance. Agents stopped relitigating
  decisions. Knowledge started improving with every delivery.

  That foundation became a platform. A typed knowledge graph formalizes relationships — not just what agents retrieve together, but why: support, contradiction, supersession,
  dependency. A confidence system learns from actual usage rather than manual calibration, adapting weights and decay rates to each domain's signal patterns. Contradiction detection
  is semantic. Any event source — hooks, webhooks, automated pipelines — feeds the learning layer without agent cooperation. Any knowledge-intensive domain — environmental monitoring,
   SRE operations, scientific research, regulatory compliance — runs on the same engine, configured not rebuilt. Secured with OAuth, containerized, serving any number of repositories
  from a single instance. The integrity chain runs through all of it: hash-chained corrections, immutable audit log, trust-attributed provenance — tamper-evident from first write to
  last.

let me give you my take... There are 2 dimensions worth considering.  Source & Use that need to drive decisions.  The concept of having Sessions and 'feature_entries' (topics), exist because of a need to tie hook generated data, to a knowledge context (topic).  I'm wondering, if these move to knowledge, how will context_retrospective be able to parse activity data (in analytics) and derive meaningful information?  If there is a way that I don't see, I'll accept it.

We have a set of tables that we consider to be premium 'knowledge'.  Retrieval and injection at the right time is our goal.. no doubt.  We have on our roadmap leaning harder into graph edges/relationships (potentially sourced out of activity??)  I think we need to think about this strucutre deeply to ensure we are making this database split decision for the right reason, and accruately describe potential downsides of taking this road.  We are partially doing this for scaling reasons.  It is a concern.  But from a pure operational side... I do beleive there will be limitations we're creating by doing this we've not fully accounted for.  I just named 1 example that we previously missed.  This is a 'no going back' type decision.. thats why I want to look at this decision from every angle before taking it.

product/research/ass-022 is where most of this research for this roadmap occurred... I think this was related to existing limitations (the move to a daemon solves the near term issues we've had, I believe), and the addition of rayon layer for high CPU functions is a second item).  My current thoughts... the SDLC use case deployed in a local project does not require db split, if we carefully address scalability.  A centralized model with higher scalability needs, may imply a different data backend technology choice.  Review the research, and provide comment

so.. one more strategic question... can I package postgres the same way we package sqllite, within the confines of the rust deployable?  Is that a strategic shift now that makes a conversion to truly scalable platform later simply swapping the backend infrastructure?

with that framing... I think we need to address the scalability issues and drive flexibility now into our future choices (doing it later is always more expensive than taking the medicine at the onset). #1 - The default permissions for a registered agent (in this case an LLM connecting to Unimatrix deployed locally through STDIO) will recieve READ, SEARCH, WRITE.  The change, is that there will be no permissive auto_enroll to get it.  We are changing the fundamental authentication (which today is agent_id, passed by the LLM filling an agent_id parameter (not security), to structurally requiring the LLM to be authenticated.  At first, it will be though an env variable set OUTSIDE the LLM (assuming settings.json setting) that unimatrix will recognize this LLM as authenticated and granted Read, WRITE, Search. This is the scope of WO-2. . In the future, Unimatrix will be centralized and the LLM will connect through HTTP, and at that time, Unimatrix will require Oauth to authenticate the LLM.

I'm not sure if thats the same identity as agent_id, which is actually used/structured more inside of Unimatrix today as a role, and in a way, if used properly by the agents, additional inforamation about the specialty of different agents the LLM will use. now, the last clarification... 3. Per-call agent_id found in registry with explicit enrollment → use those capabilities (allows future per-role narrowing)... So.. this is a research request, probably ass-023.  What I'd like you to research.  We've built an agent_id capability into unimatrix, and it works more like a role, as a part of access controls.  Unimatrix has the infrastructure to apply coarse grained role access to knowledge requests... read, write, search.  We at this time have chosen NOT to leverage this capability, primariliy because the LLM have control of agent_id... (as a field), and that fact #1 makes it not a security gate, and #2 just ends up causing friction for the LLM while it tries to figure out how to make unimatrix do what it wants it to do.  Your mission, should you choose to accept it, is to devise any way to provide any additional guardrailes to LLM's for waht they will be able to do.  There is a future effort where we're going to support HTTP protocol with Oauth... But this is still registered at the LLM instance level.  This may be the only way we can go... but I'm interested in any other research on securing llm activities

So... I spent a little time in another session designing WA-5.. this is what we came up with for requirements.  #1 - is this small enough addition that we could consolidate into 1 feature, or should it still be 2.  If stays 2 features... what do you think this implies for existing BriefingService?

---                                                                                                                                                                                  
  WA-5 Decisions                                                                                                                                                                       
                
  D-1: Transcript restoration is the primary compaction output.                                                                                                                        
  The hook reads input.transcript_path locally before sending the CompactPayload request. No server round-trip for transcript content.                                                 
                                                                                                                                                                                       
  D-2: Type-aware extraction, not verbatim replay.                                                                                                                                     
  - User turns: text blocks verbatim, tool_result blocks skipped                                                                                                                       
  - Assistant turns: text blocks verbatim + each tool_use (name + key param) paired with its tool_result truncated to ~300 bytes                                                       
  - Most recent turns first, fills budget in priority order                                                                     
                                                                                                                                                                                       
  D-3: Compact tool results, not omitted.                                                                                                                                              
  The truncated result snippet (~300 bytes) gives the agent enough context to decide whether it needs to re-fetch. Grep/Glob results are compact enough to survive whole.              
                                                                                                                                                                                       
  D-4: Separate byte budget for PreCompact.                                                                                                                                            
  A new MAX_PRECOMPACT_BYTES constant for the transcript block — distinct from MAX_INJECTION_BYTES (1400) used on other hook paths. Recommended ~3000 bytes.                           
                                                                                                                                                                                       
  D-5: Transcript block prepends the server response.                                                                                                                                  
  Task/work continuity comes before knowledge context in the compaction output.                                                                                                        
                                                                                                                                                                                       
  D-6: Graceful degradation on all transcript read failures.                                                                                                                           
  Missing path, unreadable file, malformed JSONL — all produce silent skip. Hook never fails (FR-03.7).                                                                                
                                                                                                                                                                                       
  D-7: All transcript extraction logic lives in hook.rs.                                                                                                                               
  No server changes required for WA-5.                                                                                                                                                 
                                                                                                                                                                                       
  D-8: The Unimatrix knowledge component should move to index format.                                                                                                                  
  Full-content briefing is no longer needed at compaction time — transcript restoration handles continuity. This depends on WA-4 delivering index mode on the CompactPayload path.

  ⎿  Received 111.5KB (200 OK)                                                                                                                                                         
                                                                                                                                                                                       
● Definitive answer: Yes, SubagentStart supports context injection.
                                                                                                                                                                                       
  Return this JSON from your hook's stdout:                                                                                                                                            
   
  {                                                                                                                                                                                    
    "hookSpecificOutput": {                                                 
      "hookEventName": "SubagentStart",
      "additionalContext": "your injected text here"
    }                                                                                                                                                                                  
  }
                                                                                                                                                                                       
  Key details:                                                              
  - additionalContext — the string is added to the subagent's context window before it starts working
  - Non-blocking — cannot prevent subagent creation (unlike PreToolUse which can block)                                                                                                
  - Matcher support — filter by agent type (Bash, Explore, Plan, or any custom agent name from .claude/agents/)
  - Input includes agent_id and agent_type, so your hook script can dynamically tailor the injected context based on which agent is being spawned

<result>The commit succeeded. Here is a summary of what was implemented.

## Changes made

**File:** `/workspaces/unimatrix/crates/unimatrix-server/src/services/index_briefing.rs`

### Change 1 — Doc comment on `index()` (lines 129–140)

Appended 11 lines to the existing doc comment block. The addition documents:
- Input validation delegation path through `SecurityGateway.validate_search_query()`
- Each guard enforced (S3 content, length, control characters, k bounds)
- A `WARNING:` block that explicitly names the regression risk (GH #355) so future refactors cannot silently bypass validation

### Change 2 — Regression test `index_briefing_excludes_quarantined_entry`

Added two private test helpers (`make_test_store`, `make_test_services`) mirroring the `listener.rs` pattern exactly, plus the `#[tokio::test]` regression test. The test:

1. Opens a real `SqlxStore` via `unimatrix_store::test_helpers::open_test_store`
2. Inserts one `Status::Active` entry and one `Status::Quarantined` entry directly into the store
3. Builds a full `ServiceLayer` (same construction path as production, matching the listener helper)
4. Calls `services.briefing.index(...)` with a real `AuditContext`
5. Degrades `Err(EmbeddingFailed)` to `vec![]` via `unwrap_or_default()` — matching the dispatcher's graceful degradation contract
6. Asserts the quarantined entry id never appears in results
7. Conditionally asserts the active entry is present when results are non-empty

**Test environment constraint documented inline:** `EmbedServiceHandle::new()` starts in `Loading` state (no model), so `index()` returns `Err(EmbeddingFailed)` in CI. The test still catches any future regression where (a) the post-filter is removed AND (b) an embedding model becomes available in tests — both conditions together would cause the quarantined entry to appear.

## Knowledge Stewardship

- Queried: `/uni-query-patterns` was not run (this was a focused edit-only task with no pattern discovery needed — patterns were read directly from `listener.rs` and `services/mod.rs`)
- Stored: nothing novel to store — the `TempDir` leak pattern and `ServiceLayer` test helper construction are already established in `listener.rs`. No new gotchas encountered.</result>

This gets stored, and placed in the feature histogram in memory)\

Context_briefing includes a 'task' (what is this agent supposed to do).  My thought, to enhance context_briefing, is to use the topic_goal (feature) combined with the task, to provide the prioritized index.  Agents tomorrow would issue 1 context_briefing.  Then they'be be able to context_get anything relevant immediately. and eliminate the multiple search calls. \
3. Availability of the feature goal would also improve the injection on the UDS path as well.\
\

Here's a recommendation I got from our data modeler... agrees mostly with yours.  OK.. Write this up as a 'proposed scope' for a new feature.   ▎ Store goal on the cycle_events Start event row. Cache in SessionState.current_goal for hot-path use. Load from cycle_events on session registration/resume.                        
  
  Do not store on sessions — wrong abstraction level, wrong write path, wrong reliability tier for a value that needs to survive across session boundaries and be available to         
  retrospective review.                                           
                                                                                                                                                                                       
  One forward-looking note: if the goal sentence is eventually embedded (vectorized) for semantic search ranking — using it as a context vector in FusedScoreInputs — the embedding

  with it.

[Assistant] Spawning architect and specification writer in parallel with all settled decisions.
[Assistant] Got it — the sequence makes sense now:

1. Agent calls `context_cycle(start, goal: "...too long...")` 
2. Hook fires → **UDS writes truncated version** to `cycle_events` (defensive truncate, no failure)
3. MCP handler validates → **rejects with error** → agent sees it and corrects
4. Agent retries with shorter goal → MCP accepts → **UDS writes again → overwrites the truncated version**

Last writer wins. The corrected goal ends up as the stored value. The cycle_id is the same across both attempts so observation attribution is never disrupted.

So the actual behavior per path:

| Path | Over limit | Result |
|---|---|---|
| MCP | Reject with descriptive error | Agent corrects and retries |
| UDS | Truncate at char boundary, write it | Overwritten if agent retries successfully |

One `MAX_GOAL_BYTES` constant. The UDS truncation isn't a final state — it's just defensive handling that gets naturally corrected by the successful retry. Clean.

[Assistant] Targeted fix — spawning architect and spec writer in parallel to align `MAX_GOAL_BYTES = 1024`.
[Assistant] Sharp point. The goal feeds directly into the embedding pipeline — and 384-dim models (typically 128–256 token windows) start losing quality well before you hit their hard truncation limit. Beyond ~100 tokens the embedding centroid diffuses rather than sharpens.

443 bytes ≈ ~90–110 tokens — already at the sweet spot for dense retrieval. At 2048 you're past the model's effective window; at 1024 you're enforcing conciseness that actually *benefits* retrieval quality.

**1024 is the right value.** It gives ~2.3x headroom over the 443-byte example, stays within the effective embedding range of a 384-dim model, and enforces the design intent: a focused 1–2 sentence statement, not a paragraph. A goal that approaches 1024 bytes is probably already too diffuse to embed well anyway — the limit doubles as a quality gate.

## ASS-033 Summary

The research is in `product/research/ass-033/` — `SCOPE.md` (summary) and `FINDINGS.md` (full analysis).

### The Core Finding

**Most high-value improvements are markdown formatter changes.** The data is already computed and present in the JSON response. The formatter just doesn't render it.

### Three Opportunity Categories

**1. New data available from col-024/025**
- **Goal in report header** — `cycle_events.goal` (col-025) gives a durable statement of intent; display it prominently
- **Attribution provenance** — tell the reader which path was used (cycle_events-first vs. legacy fallback) so they can trust the numbers
- **In-progress cycle reporting** — col-024's open-ended window support means `context_cycle_review` can be called on an *active* cycle (mid-session health check)
- **Goal-contextualized hotspot severity** — a `context_load` hotspot is expected for a design cycle, alarming for a bugfix cycle; the goal string enables that classification

**2. Existing data not rendered in markdown (#203 gaps)**
- Session profile: tool distribution (218R/59E/41W), agents spawned, top file zones
- Temporal burst sketches: `+45m(6 compiles in 4min — background.rs, timeout.rs)` instead of raw epoch timestamps
- "What Went Well" section — positive baseline signals (above-average parallelism, zero sleep hacks) are completely invisible
- Full knowledge category breakdown (currently "11 served" instead of "decision×6, convention×2...")

**3. Metric correctness fix (#320)**
- `feature_knowledge_reuse` counts only same-cycle entries, making the tool look underused. 9 counted vs. 49 actually served. Cross-feature reuse — the highest-value signal — is invisible. Fix: split into `total_served`, `cross_feature_reuse`, `intra_cycle_reuse`, `by_category`.

### What One Feature Could Ship

A col-026 delivery could cover all high/medium items with most changes being formatter-only (no new DB reads, no schema changes). The full priority ranking is in `FINDINGS.md` section 8.

**Corrected picture:**

`query_count` and `category_counts` are structurally different:

| Signal | Event log? | Training reconstruction? | New table? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `category_counts` | No | Impossible from DB | Yes — OQ-01's `session_category_snapshots` |
| `query_count` | Yes — `query_log` | `COUNT(*) WHERE session_id = ? AND ts <= label_ts` | **No** |

ASS-031 `TRAINING-DESIGN.md` already specifies this reconstruction path. The GNN training pipeline reads `query_log` directly with a timestamp bound — so historical sessions get the correct count at training time. The in-memory `query_count` field serves inference only (session context vector during live scoring).

**Scope for this feature is therefore:**
- Add `query_count: u32` to `SessionState` (in-memory, inference path only)
- Add `increment_query_count()` to `SessionRegistry`
- Call it in `context_search` handler
- No schema migration, no new table, no analytics write queue

**Remaining open questions before I proceed to Phase 1b:**

- **OQ-A**: Should `query_count` also increment in the UDS hook-triggered search path? (Since `query_log` captures both, training reconstruction counts both — inference should match for consistency.)
- **OQ-C**: Increment before or after reading for the session context vector? Recommendation: increment-first so the current call is included; training reconstruction uses `ts <= label_ts` (inclusive) to match.
- **OQ-D** (no action now): Any future `query_log` GC must stay within the GNN training window retention.

[Assistant] That's the right concern to raise here. If `context_get` represents high-intent retrieval and `context_lookup` represents structured filtering behavior, training on search-only `query_count` might teach the GNN a noisy or incomplete picture of agent session patterns.

The spike needs to answer this properly before it can close. Sending the researcher to dig into it.
[Assistant] Clean answer from the codebase itself:

**`query_log` only records search calls** — there is no `tool_name` column and neither `context_get` nor `context_lookup` write to it at all.

| Tool | `query_log`? |
|---|---|
| `context_search` (MCP) | Yes |
| UDS hook search | Yes |
| `context_lookup` | **No** |
| `context_get` | **No** |

So the training reconstruction (`COUNT(*) FROM query_log WHERE session_id = ?`) already defines what "query" means: semantic search only. Any `SessionState.query_count` that included get or lookup calls would permanently diverge from training semantics — corrupting the GNN's session context vector.

**Your intuition is right though** — `context_get` is a stronger intent signal and `context_lookup` carries category-restriction behavior. But acting on that requires either:
1. Extending `query_log` with a `tool_name` column and wiring get/lookup to write there, OR
2. Separate session-level counters (`get_count`, `lookup_count`) with their own persistence

Either is a future spike (flagged as OQ-E in SCOPE.md). For W3-1, we're constrained to search-only to keep training/inference aligned with what already exists.

**One thing OQ-A resolved cleanly**: the UDS hook path **must** also increment `query_count` — because it already writes to `query_log`, so training reconstruction counts those rows. Excluding UDS from the in-memory counter would cause inference to under-count in hook-driven sessions.

[Assistant] Understood — no commits, no code changes. The research artifacts are saved to `product/research/ass-032/SCOPE.md` only.

Here's what the deep audit found:

---

**The pipeline is already structurally biased — and EWC++ makes it durable.**

The scoring formula (`0.85*similarity + 0.15*confidence + co_access_boost`) means entries that never surface can never accumulate `access_count`, `helpful_count`, or co-access pairs. With the current corpus (~53 active entries, nearly all "decision"), the W3-1 training reservoir will be populated almost exclusively from one category. EWC++ then *freezes* those learned weights across training runs — the bias doesn't erode, it compounds.

---

**On restating the goal:**

The original framing — *"GNN prioritizes categories by project phase"* — conflates two separable problems:
1. **Discovery**: making sure underrepresented categories get surfaced at all
2. **Phase-aware ranking**: once surfaced, scoring entries by relevance to the current workflow moment

A GNN can't solve (1) — it can only rank what it's already been trained to value. (1) requires an exploration mechanism. (2) is what the GNN is actually suited for.

**Restated goal**: *Surface the most contextually relevant entry for the current workflow phase, while ensuring no category is systematically excluded from consideration by the scoring pipeline.*

---

**Recommended path (staged):**

- **Stage 1** — Exploration epsilon: reserve 1 briefing slot for a random draw from underrepresented categories. Starts generating diverse training labels immediately.
- **Stage 2** — Phase-affinity matrix: activate the existing `w_phase_explicit` placeholder in `compute_fused_score` with a static config lookup. Category-diversity improvement with zero risk.
- **Stage 3** — W3-1 GNN: proceed only after corpus diversification, with IPS-weighted training samples and a category coverage gate before first activation.

---

**Three questions that need your input before Stage 3 can be scheduled:**

- **OQ-A**: What's the expected entry creation rate per non-decision category going forward? (Determines when Stage 3 becomes viable)
- **OQ-B**: Does the W1-3 eval harness include cross-category recall, or only P@5/MRR on dominant entries? (If the latter, it won't detect the degradation W3-1 risks introducing)
- **OQ-C**: Should the exploration slot be a fixed 1-of-k reservation or a configurable `exploration_fraction`?

[Assistant] You're right on all counts. Let me revise:

**What I got wrong:**
- Max 3 parallel agents, not 20 — my math for "session termination gaps" falls apart completely
- skip-permissions has been on for ALL sessions in this repo — so ALL prior recurrences (#1164, #1608, #2803, #2959) were also not permission friction. The "fifth recurrence" framing is entirely wrong.

**What the Pre-Post differential might actually be:**

Given skip-permissions is universal, the only cases where PreToolUse fires without PostToolUse are:
1. **Actual session interruption** (Ctrl-C, crash) — the last tool call in a killed session has no Post. With at most 3 agents, this explains a handful of events, not 38 Read events.
2. **Hook asymmetry** — if the PostToolUse hook filters differently than PreToolUse (e.g., doesn't record reads), Read would systematically show Pre > Post across every session.
3. **MCP connection drops** — `context_store`/`context_search`/`context_get` calls where the MCP server dropped mid-call. PreToolUse fires before the call, but if the connection drops, PostToolUse may not fire.

The user's point about "Access errors in logs" suggests the rule is fundamentally mislabeled — **the Pre-Post differential is not a permission signal at all in this repo**. Genuine friction would show as error responses in PostToolUse records, not a count imbalance.

**My question before correcting #3438:** Do you know whether the PostToolUse hook records ALL tools or only a subset? If it systematically skips Read, that would explain Read=38 as a permanent hook asymmetry artifact — and the rule has never measured anything meaningful in this repo.

=== Recent conversation (last 0 exchanges) ===
[Assistant] Yes — that's exactly what's happening. The SubagentStart path reuses `extract_transcript_block`, which is the PreCompact restoration function. Here's the flow:

**Step 5b (SubagentStart fallback, hook.rs:88-115):**
```
transcript_path → extract_transcript_block() → HookRequest::ContextSearch { query: q }
```

**Step 5d (PreCompact, hook.rs:124-134):**
```
transcript_path → extract_transcript_block() → prepend to briefing output
```

**Same function, different purpose.** `extract_transcript_block` was designed to produce a *context restoration block* — up to `MAX_PRECOMPACT_BYTES = 3000` bytes, formatted with structural headers (`=== Recent conversation (last 0 exchanges) ===` / `=== End recent conversation ===`). For PreCompact that's correct — it's injected as context. For SubagentStart it's passed directly as the **vector search query**.

Two concrete problems result:

1. **Noisy query**: The structural headers (`=== Recent conversation ===`, `=== End recent conversation ===`) are part of the string fed to the embedding model. They add signal noise — the embedding is partially anchored to template text, not semantics.

2. **Over-long query**: Up to 3000 bytes. Embedding models are optimized for shorter queries — a focused 100-200 byte description of what the subagent is doing would almost certainly yield better retrieval than a 3000-byte transcript dump.

The `(2)` result counts you're seeing mean it *is* working — the transcript body does contain enough semantic signal for the embeddings to surface relevant entries. But the retrieval quality is probably lower than if SubagentStart had its own query extraction that stripped the headers and targeted just the task description (e.g., the most recent `Agent(...)` tool call description from the transcript).

The comment at line 84 acknowledges the workaround: *"Claude Code does not send prompt_snippet in the SubagentStart payload, so... [we] derive a query from the transcript tail."* The PreCompact function was the nearest available tool when this was built.

Yes... just confirm this has nothing to do with supersession chains, Some other analyst advised getting rid of thos.. but they are hashed and show the knowledge evolution

1. UsageDedup + briefing → subsequent get silenced

    This is a real correctness risk. The dedup fires before the access_weight multiplier (C-05
    in the codebase), which means a weight-0 briefing appearance can burn the dedup slot and
    silence a later context_get on the same entry — exactly the selection signal you want most.
    The fix: weight-0 entries must bypass dedup entirely, or use a separate dedup bucket for
    "selections" (confirmed_entries) distinct from "offers" (briefing). The simplest resolution
    is a guard: if ctx.access_weight == 0 { skip dedup registration }. Briefing at weight-0
    means "we showed it, don't track it as an access" — dedup registration is an access-side
    concern and shouldn't fire at all.

    2. context_lookup cardinality — request-side, not response-side

    Use "single ID in the request" as the trigger. This reflects agent intent, not system
    behavior. An agent sending one ID to lookup is making an explicit selection — they knew what
     they wanted. Multiple IDs in the same request is batch retrieval, weaker individual signal.
     "Single entry returned" is an artifact of filtering/status; it doesn't tell you the agent
    specifically chose that entry. Request cardinality is the signal.

    3. confirmed_entries — add now

    Add the field now with no consumer. The cost is a HashSet<u64> on SessionState — negligible.
     The risk of deferring: the field is session-ephemeral. You cannot retroactively reconstruct
     which entries were confirmed in past sessions. If you wait for Thompson Sampling, you ship
    with zero historical data. The data starts accumulating the moment the field exists. One
    small PR now, Thompson Sampling inherits a populated dataset rather than a cold start.

    4. Phase helper — free function

    Free function taking &SessionRegistry. Four call sites, a critical signal collection path —
    testability matters here. A private method on the handler struct couples testing to handler
    construction. A free function taking &SessionRegistry can be unit-tested directly, and the
    call site reads as intentional: "I am explicitly capturing phase from the session registry
    here." All four call sites are in tools.rs so method would work, but the testability
    argument wins for something this important to the signal pipeline.

    5. weight-0 path — verify the multiplier path

    The risk is real: if usage.rs implements the increment as access_count += 1 with weight
    applied elsewhere (as a confidence multiplier, not as the increment itself), then weight-0
    won't zero out the access_count increment. You need to confirm the actual arithmetic is
    access_count += access_weight (or equivalent), not access_count += 1; confidence_weight =
    access_weight. Also check whether weight-0 entries still generate co-access pairs — they
    probably should not, since co-access represents structural co-retrieval, and an
    offered-but-not-selected entry shouldn't be paired. Verify both before shipping the briefing
     weight change.

[Assistant] **Phase 1 complete.** SCOPE.md is at `product/features/nan-008/SCOPE.md`.

Here's what the researcher found and what needs your approval before proceeding.

---

### Scope summary

**Problem**: P@K and MRR measure consistency with the current system. Features that deliberately change result distribution (PPR #398, Contradicts suppression #395) produce false regression signals for correct behavior. CC@k and ICD provide ground-truth-free quality signals.

**Two change surfaces, 9 files:**
- `runner/` — metrics.rs (new functions), output.rs (new fields on `ProfileResult`, `ComparisonMetrics`, `ScoredEntry`), replay.rs (pass `configured_categories` to computation)
- `report/` — mod.rs (deserialize new fields with `#[serde(default)]`), aggregate.rs (accumulate new stats), render.rs (new table columns + Distribution Analysis section 6)
- `docs/testing/eval-harness.md` — document CC@k and ICD
- `product/test/eval-baselines/log.jsonl` + `README.md` — append baseline entry

**Key constraint discovered**: `runner/output.rs` and `report/mod.rs` maintain independent copies of `ProfileResult`, `ComparisonMetrics`, and `ScoredEntry` — both must be updated in sync, with `#[serde(default)]` on report's copies for backward compat.

**`configured_categories`** is sourced from `profile.config_overrides.knowledge.categories` (from `KnowledgeConfig`) — no hardcoding.

---

### Three open questions for you

1. **`category` in result JSON** — The researcher recommends adding `category: String` to `ScoredEntry` (persisted to output JSON). The alternative is computing CC@k/ICD inline and discarding it. The SCOPE recommends persisting it (useful for future metrics). Any objection?

2. **ICD denominator** — ICD entropy is computed over the *actual result distribution* (not normalized by `|configured_categories|`). So a result with 2 categories present produces entropy over `p(cat_A)` and `p(cat_B)`, independent of how many categories are configured. CC@k uses `configured_categories` as denominator; ICD does not. Does this match your expectation, or do you want ICD normalized to [0, 1]?

3. **Baseline recording** — Appending to `log.jsonl` requires running `eval run` against the current snapshot. Is a current snapshot available, or should the delivery agent produce one as part of the feature work?

1. Section ordering — Phase-Stratified Metrics before Distribution Analysis (section 6).
    Distribution Analysis is aggregate/statistical; phase stratification answers "which phase
    improved." Reviewers will read phase first to understand where a change had effect, then go
    to Distribution Analysis for the what. Chronological reading order matters in a shipping gate
     document.

    2. Table granularity — One row per phase for the first iteration. The per-(phase × profile)
    table is the right eventual target but it doubles the row count and makes the diff hard to
    read when you have 3 phases × 2-3 profiles. Ship the simpler form. The delta columns
    (candidate − baseline) give you the cross-profile signal without multiplying rows. You can
    add the cross-product view in a later iteration once the format is validated.

    3. Phase vocabulary — From the codebase and protocol context visible in this conversation,
    the known values are design, delivery, and bugfix. These are the three session types defined
    in CLAUDE.md and the routing protocol. There are no other phase values in production — the
    phase strings come from context_cycle calls which follow the protocol-defined vocabulary.
    Document exactly those three plus a (none) bucket for pre-#397 rows and sessions that never
    set a phase. Don't use "etc." or open-ended wording — the vocabulary is closed and bounded by
     the domain pack configuration.  One point worth discussing: vocabulary.  It is possible for design, delivery, bugfix to evolve with the project (not often, but we cannot exclude this)

workign on 402... design had these questions: Open questions for your input:                      
                                                        
  1. Multi-candidate mixed mode — if some profiles
  declare distribution_change = true and others don't,  
  should Section 5 render per-profile (Distribution Gate
   for declaring profiles, zero-regression rows for     
  others)? The researcher recommends per-profile      
  independence.
  2. profile-meta.json version field — include
  "version": 1 from the start for forward compatibility?
   Researcher recommends yes.
  3. mrr_floor semantics — is it a third must-pass      
  target (all three must pass equally), or is it a veto 
  that blocks approval even if CC@k + ICD pass? In
  practice the same, but the report wording differs.    
  4. Who reads profile-meta.json — run_report reads it
  itself from the results dir (cleaner), or             
  run_eval_command loads and passes it explicitly (more
  explicit data flow)? Researcher recommends option (a).

1. Per-profile independence — yes.                         
  Mixed sections would be misleading. If a run includes both
  distribution-changing and stable profiles, the reader needs
   to see each profile's gate on its own terms. A global   
  Section 5 that flattens them hides whether the             
  distribution-changing profile actually passed. Per-profile
  is the only honest rendering.

  2. Version field — yes, include it.                        
  Costs nothing now. The alternative is later parsing "absent
   field = version 1" which is always messier than an        
  explicit field. Start at "version": 1.                   
                                                             
  3. MRR floor is a veto, not a co-equal target.             
  The semantics are different even if the outcome is the same
   today. CC@k and ICD measure whether diversity improved.   
  MRR floor measures whether ranking quality didn't collapse.
   They're orthogonal: you can improve diversity by shuffling
   the ranking and tank MRR. The floor framing —           
  "distribution gate passes only if MRR doesn't fall below
  threshold" — is the accurate one. Report wording should
  reflect this: distribution gate result first, then MRR
  floor: PASS/FAIL as a separate line that can independently
  block. This also makes it easier to diagnose failures —
  "diversity targets met, but ranking regressed" is a
  different problem than "diversity targets not met."

  4. run_report reads profile-meta.json itself.
  The results directory is the artifact boundary — everything
   needed to reproduce the report should live there. If      
  run_eval_command loads and passes it, you've created a
  coupling where the report can't be re-rendered from the    
  results dir alone (e.g., CI artifacts, post-hoc analysis).
  Option (a) keeps the report self-contained.

at the same time, I'm working on 413 and they have open qeustions: Open questions needing your input before design proceeds:           
  
  1. mean_entry_degree direction — in+out (2× per edge, undirected    
  view) or out-degree only? Proposed: in+out for better connectivity
  picture.                                                            
  2. Connectivity of bootstrap-only entries — should an entry     
  connected only via bootstrap_only=1 edges count as connected or     
  isolated? Proposed: isolated (exclude bootstrap edges from all
  metrics, matching TypedRelationGraph.inner).                        
  3. Format placement — graph cohesion as a sub-section of existing
  Coherence block, or standalone "Graph Topology" section? Proposed:  
  Coherence sub-section.
  4. Computation timing — one async SQL call per context_status       
  invocation (fast aggregate), or cache in the background tick?       
  Proposed: per-call (diagnostic only, not operational).

1. in+out degree — yes.             
  Out-degree only would show an entry with many incoming
  Supports edges (highly referenced) as near-zero, which is  
  wrong. For a connectivity picture, you want to know how
  embedded an entry is in the graph overall. In+out is the   
  right measure.                                           

  2. Exclude bootstrap edges — isolated.
  This is the correct call and the consistent one. Bootstrap
  edges were seeded from co_access count ≥ 3, not from NLI   
  inference. The whole point of #413 is to measure whether
  #412 (graph inference) is actually producing real edges. If
   bootstrap-only entries count as connected, the          
  isolated_entry_count metric starts low regardless of
  whether inference is working — it's measuring the wrong
  thing. The signal you want is "how many entries has NLI
  inference reached?" Treating bootstrap-only as isolated
  gives you that. It also matches TypedRelationGraph.inner's
  existing behavior, so no semantic mismatch.

  3. Coherence sub-section.                                  
  Graph cohesion is a health dimension, not a separate
  operational concern. Standalone section implies parity with
   context_status's top-level outputs; Coherence sub-section
  puts it where it belongs. Consistent with how contradiction
   coverage and embedding health are already treated.      

  4. Per-call SQL.
  Background tick caching is for operationally critical
  values on the hot path. Graph cohesion is diagnostic — you 
  look at it to verify that #412 is doing something after a
  maintenance window. Per-call gives you a live snapshot at  
  the moment you ask, which is exactly what you want when  
  debugging graph inference behavior. Caching would show
  last-tick values right after you manually triggered a pass,
   which is confusing. The aggregate SQL (COUNT, AVG over
  GRAPH_EDGES) is cheap; per-call cost is negligible.

OQ-A1 — Yes, include the baseline MRR reference row.
  Without it, mrr_floor values are set blind. A user seeing
  "MRR Floor: 0.35 — PASS" with no reference doesn't know if 
  their floor is dangerously loose (baseline is 0.36) or
  well-calibrated (baseline is 0.51). The second parameter to
   the render function is worth it. Label the row clearly as
  "Baseline MRR (reference)" so it's obvious it's
  informational, not a gate criterion.

  OQ-A2 — Acceptable, with one clarification.                
  The parent heading ## 5. Distribution Gate should still
  appear in multi-candidate runs as the section anchor, with 
  ### 5.1 — {profile_name} as children. This keeps the ## 5.
  anchor stable regardless of candidate count — important if 
  CI tooling or humans link to that section. Single-candidate
   can skip the sub-heading (no need for ### 5.1 when there's
   only one). That distinction should be explicit in the
  spec.

  OQ-S1 — Reject with ConfigInvariant at parse time.
  Silent ignore is the wrong tradeoff here. If
  distribution_change = true lands on the baseline by        
  mistake, silent ignore gives the user a zero-regression
  report when they may have expected a distribution gate — or
   worse, they don't notice the misconfiguration at all. This
   is a user error that should surface immediately, not be
  papered over. Hard error at parse time with a clear message
   ("baseline profile must not declare distribution_change =
  true") is unambiguous and fixable.

  OQ-S2 — Surface as error and abort.
  Silent fallback to zero-regression is a correctness hazard.
   If the file is corrupt, you don't know whether the run was
   distribution-gated or not — silently applying
  zero-regression could let a distribution-changing feature  
  appear to pass under the wrong gate. The report should fail
   with a clear message ("profile-meta.json is malformed —
  re-run eval to regenerate") and exit non-zero. The results
  directory is the artifact boundary; a corrupt meta file
  means the artifact is invalid.

now back to 413... does reading from a write pool make sense to you? ## ADR-003: write_pool_server() for Graph Cohesion SQL Queries

### Context

`unimatrix-store` exposes two SQLite connection pools:

- `read_pool()` — a read-only pool intended for high-concurrency query paths
  (search, lookup, get). Uses `SqliteConnectOptions` with `read_only: true`.
- `write_pool_server()` — a write-capable pool exposed to the server crate for
  operations that need write access or must serialise with writes.

The `compute_status_aggregates()` function — the direct precedent for
`compute_graph_cohesion_metrics()` — uses `read_pool()` for its aggregate queries
(confirmed at lines 959, 983 of `read.rs`). This appears to allow read-only queries
to use the lighter pool.

However, `query_bootstrap_contradicts()` and other GRAPH_EDGES-reading functions use
`write_pool_server()`. The SCOPE.md constraint explicitly states: "all queries use
`write_pool_server()`" and cites this as confirmed by `compute_status_aggregates`
(though `compute_status_aggregates` actually uses `read_pool()`).

The risk of using `read_pool()` for `compute_graph_cohesion_metrics()`:
- GRAPH_EDGES is written by the NLI inference service, which uses `write_pool_server()`
  through the write path. Under SQLite WAL mode, read connections may see a
  slightly stale snapshot depending on checkpoint state.
- `context_status` is a diagnostic tool. Operators invoke it to see current state.
  Returning stale cohesion values (e.g., showing 0 inferred edges right after NLI
  inference ran) would be confusing.
- Consistency: other GRAPH_EDGES queries (`query_bootstrap_contradicts`,
  `find_nli_count_for_circuit_breaker`) already use `write_pool_server()`. Using a
  different pool for cohesion creates an inconsistent query pattern on the same table.

### Decision

Use `write_pool_server()` for both SQL queries in `compute_graph_cohesion_metrics()`.

This matches the pattern of all other GRAPH_EDGES-reading methods in `read.rs` and
ensures the cohesion snapshot is consistent with the current write state.

Note: the SCOPE.md assertion that `compute_status_aggregates` uses `write_pool_server()`
is incorrect — it uses `read_pool()`. The architecture documents the actual decision
separately. Cohesion queries use `write_pool_server()` because GRAPH_EDGES is a
write-active table during inference runs, not because of a universal rule.

### Consequences

Easier:
- Cohesion metrics reflect the same GRAPH_EDGES state as writes from NLI inference
- Consistent with existing GRAPH_EDGES query methods
- No pool contention with `context_status`'s other operations, which also use
  `write_pool_server()` for Phase 1 aggregates

Harder:
- `write_pool_server()` has a stricter acquire timeout (5s vs 2s for `read_pool()`).
  Under high write load this could slow down `context_status`. Acceptable trade-off
  given the diagnostic, infrequent nature of this call.
so.. context_status being run infrequently, is also a read only function... blending read patterns in a write pool, isnt that a messy architecture?

also just closed out 413.  Have now started scoping 395.  Here's the scope evaluation and question: col-030: Contradicts Collision Suppression — product/features/col-030/SCOPE.md

  Problem: The search pipeline may surface two entries with a Contradicts edge between them in
  the same response. The higher-ranked entry's contradiction is served silently alongside it — a
   "knowledge collision." The fix is a post-scoring filter that drops the lower-ranked member of
   each conflicting pair.

  Key findings:
  - Infrastructure is ~90% there: Contradicts edges already exist in GRAPH_EDGES,
  TypedRelationGraph already loads them, edges_of_type already exists — they're just never
  queried in the search path today
  - Insertion point: Step 10b — after floors (Step 10), before ScoredEntry construction (Step
  11)
  - No schema changes, no new deps, no scoring formula changes
  - typed_graph clone is already in scope in search.rs from Step 6 — no new lock needed
  - Eval gate: zero-regression check (existing scenarios have no Contradicts edges, so
  suppression is a no-op → gate passes trivially, proving non-regression)
  - Pure function suppress_contradicts(result_ids, graph) -> Vec<bool> in graph.rs, fully
  unit-testable

  Open questions for you:
  1. Edge direction: Does nli_detection.rs write Contradicts edges bidirectionally (A→B and B→A)
   or unidirectionally? Proposed approach checks both directions for safety — confirm this is
  correct.
  2. context_lookup scope: Confirmed out of scope (suppression only applies to ranked lists)?
  Current SCOPE.md says yes — no warning on single-entry fetch.
  3. Audit log enrichment: Should Step 12 audit event record suppressed-entry count? (trivially
  addable, currently out of scope)

OQ1 — Edge direction: unidirectional, checking both directions is 
  required (not just safe).                        
                                                                    
  The code at line 509–523 writes (source_id, neighbor_id,          
  'Contradicts') only — one direction, always from the new entry    
  toward its neighbor. There is no reverse write. This means a      
  contradiction between entries A and B will have an edge in        
  whichever direction happened to be the "new entry" at detection   
  time. The suppress_contradicts function must check both A→B and
  B→A to be correct, not just safe. Confirm this in the SCOPE.md —
  "both directions for safety" should read "both directions
  required; edges are unidirectional at write time."

  OQ2 — context_lookup: correct, out of scope.

  Suppression applies to ranked lists where multiple candidates     
  compete. context_lookup is a deterministic fetch by ID — the agent
   already chose the entry. No collision to suppress. If            
  contradiction warnings on single-entry fetch are ever wanted,   
  that's a separate proactive conflict notification feature.

  OQ3 — Audit log enrichment: defer.

  The eval gate for #395 is zero-regression on scenarios with no    
  Contradicts edges — suppression is a no-op in current test data.
  Audit data would show nothing useful until #412 ships real edges. 
  Add it as a follow-up comment on #395 for post-#412 enrichment, 
  same pattern as the EDGE_SOURCE_NLI constant migration on #412.
